title: Hamnet and the 2026 Best Adapted Screenplay Race
text: Sasha Stone defends the film Hamnet against common misinterpretations, particularly responding to Ian McKellen's dismissive comments. She argues the central misunderstanding is that the film attempts to explain how Shakespeare got the idea for Hamlet, when it actually serves a different purpose entirely. Stone contends that Hamnet celebrates art's transformative power. She writes: The point of Hamnet is to celebrate art. It is about how art takes us out of human tragedy and leads us to catharsis. Rather than a biographical explanation, she frames it as an imaginative work inspired by Maggie O'Farrell's novel—one that explores how personal tragedy becomes creative material. Stone acknowledges the adapted screenplay race should be competitive, but predicts One Battle After Another will prevail, having already secured the WGA Scripter award. She notes Hamnet had momentum at one point but has since lost ground in the competition. Stone emphasizes that Hamnet differs fundamentally from films like Shakespeare in Love—it doesn't romanticize Shakespeare's life but rather examines grief and artistic expression through a minimalist lens directed by Chloé Zhao.
